chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: The first thing I would like to tell you, which I did not mention the last time,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: that in the reading assignment, the syllabus that we handed out,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: and by the way, does everybody have one?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: If you don't, we have some extras here.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: You all have one. Good.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Anyway, as you can see on the first page, there is a section called preparatory reading.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So I would recommend that if you can, to each time read this recommended reading,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: which is available, of course, on the internet, and that will make it easier for you to follow the lecture,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: because this course, as both in vision and audition, relies on imparting a great many facts about the work news of these two systems.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And it's easier to be able to memorize these facts by doing the preparatory reading if you can.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: All right, so today, then, what we are going to do is we're going to initially start to talk about the layout of the visual system,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: and then we're going to talk about the retina for much of the course, and at the very end,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: we'll talk a little bit about the lateral genetically nucleus.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: In each case, I will try to tell you about how some people have thought about things,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: and what the progression is of the discoveries that have been made about the visual system.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: It is certainly an area of research, which has been incredibly successful, and has resulted in quite a number of noble prizes for the discoveries that individuals had made in uncovering the workings of the system.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Furthermore, many of the uncoverings that investigators had done came about as incredible surprises,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: because so many of the findings were quite unexpected.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And I will try to point these things out during the course.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And my section, as I've mentioned before, will consist of 12 lectures, and then that will be followed on October 23 by a midterm exam,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: and again, to reiterate that's going to consist of a series of multiple choice questions, and then following that,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: we'll move on to talk about the auditory system that Chris Brown is going to be presenting to you.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: All right, so the first thing I want to do then is to talk about the basic wiring of the visual system,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: which in itself has yielded a number of unexpected surprises, and also raised some interesting questions.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So what I want to start with, first of all, is that when you look at some animals that have sideways-looking eyes, like the rabbit, and most amphibious,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: the two eyes look out in two different directions, to the left and to the right, and that enables them to see a very large portion of the visual field.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And when that is the case, as in this picture shown here for the rabbit, what you find is that there's only a small area which is seen by both eyes.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: But then when we look at higher mammals, in particularly primates and humans, what you find that there was a huge change in the course of evolution,

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: bringing the eyes to the front. Now, that change is very interesting, and we can ask, why on earth did that happen?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Because it meant that obviously a human cannot see behind themselves where these rabbits can see a much larger portion of the visual field.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so you sacrifice the ability to see all the way around the world for being able to see with both eyes to the front.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so the question is, why did this happen?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now we are going to discuss this in some detail, because one of the prime reasons, I believe at least, that this has happened is to be able to process information about depth better than it can be done by rabbits and other animals.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so when we talk about depth perception, we are going to deal with this issue in more detail.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, if you look at an animal like a rabbit, what you find and also animals, which, as I've mentioned already, sorry, I'm going backwards here, that you also have efficient amphibia.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: You have these sideways looking eyes, and now we can ask the question, how do these two eyes make their connections through the ganglion cells to the central nervous system?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that in fact, it was quite an issue at one time, believe it or not, before an atomic technique became more sophisticated.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And the reason it became an issue is because it was discovered predominantly actually by Cajal, who I have mentioned had received the Nobel Prize for the beautiful work he had done on not only the vision, but in general about the nervous system in 1906 with Golgi.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: He discovered, as did several other investigators, that the optic nerve crosses over at the Sokrat Chiasm, and so the input from the left eye projects into the right half of the brain and from the right eye to the left half of the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that became a huge issue. Why on earth would this happen?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And we are going to deal with this repeatedly. It's a very interesting issue. And even more interesting is the fact that when the eyes move to the front, what happened is that this kind of connection became much more complicated.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: But before I do that, let me just say a few more words here. First of all, that in fission amphibia, you have a huge structure called the optic tectum, which is the primary visual processing center in the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And there is also the lateral genocular nucleus of the thalamus in these animals, but that's quite small and not very well developed. And there is a cortex in these animals, but it is again a primitive area compared to primates, for example.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And the lateral genocular nucleus projects to the cortex. So the left cortex in these animals gets input from the right eye and the opposite is the case for the right cortex.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so consequently, this cortex sees this part of the visual field and this cortex sees this part of the visual field. Like one was like an inversion.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, you come to what happened as a result of the eyes moving to the front. When the eyes move to the front in the course of evolution that necessitated a major rewiring in the visual system.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So that's what we are going to look at next, because that's us. So what we have here then, we have the two eyes looking to the front. And imagine that these two eyes now looking at something here is a dot here and both eyes are looking at the same dot so that you only see a single dot.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So now what happens is really something surprising. What happens is that the, let me interrupt for a second and tell you that imagine that you cut your eye, I mean, don't do it, imagine that you cut your eye vertically in half each eye.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that means that each eye has, if you will, a nasal half and a temporal half. All right. So here we have the two eyes. Here's a left eye and what we have is a nasal half, okay.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So the right eye projects across and the same thing is true for the right eye projects across. But now if you look at the temporal hemorrhemy, hemorrhemy, remember that's the word for it. Something very interesting happens.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: The temporal hemorrhemy, don't cross over, but they remain on the same side. So we have a color code here that the red parts project across and the black parts also project across from one eye, but it's the Ypsilettle for the other eye.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So you say, my God, why did this happen? Why don't earth is going on? And then if you study this, you realize that this is actually one of the few truly logical things that you encounter when you study the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And you're not going to be able to study the brain, or not don't seem to be too logical because in the force of evolution, you have to change things in very peculiar subtle ways to make things work. You couldn't just redo the whole system from scratch.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So there's certainly a logical one, and I'll explain it to you in just a minute. Okay, so now what we do next is that again we have the two lateral geniclet nuclei, but now these are in the thalamus.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So, you know, I have grown tremendously in size and became much more important in visual processing, as did then the cortex, this process by the way is called encephalization.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So in primitive animals, you have very small cortex, and then the course of evolution, the cortex grew and grew and grew and grew and became more and more important structure in being able to analyze just about anything and certainly analyze vision.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now here we have a cortical area in the posterior part of the brain, as I had pointed out to you the last time, and what we have is a whole series of visual areas in the cortex.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So, the most central one I guess would be area V1, the primary visual cortex, to which the lateral geniclet projects most profusely. Then we have a whole bunch of other visual areas as we'll discuss in more detail later on.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And then what happens is that we have several other cortical areas, so cortex I think you all of you know that, is divided into four lobes. Okay, the frontal, temporal, parietal, and the V1 is called the occipital lobes.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, the visual areas that you see in the back here make extensive interconnections and connections to these other lobes in the brain to be able to analyze the visual scene.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, in addition, what we have, still, as we had the mofrimative animals, we have a superior coliculus, actually two of them, one on each side, and then we have another set of areas called the terminal nuclei, the nucleus of the optic track, about which we'll just talk a little bit, because there are specializing in certain visual functions, but not nearly as compelling and interesting as the work that it has been done in the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: The cortical areas and in the superior coliculi. So this is the arrangement, but now we want to understand why do you have this strange connection here.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So to understand that, what we are going to talk about is the so-called choropter or the V-smiller circle. Now, what does that? Somebody, some clever guy came up with this observation.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: They made a circle that goes through the nodal point of the eye, and it is diameter depends on where you are fixating. So fixating here, all right?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so in this case, the red area, which is your left visual hemifield, impinges on the nasal retina of the left eye and the temporal retina of the other eye.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So if you have an object here, all right, that hits corresponding retinal elements in the two eyes.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that is the basic rule. Anywhere along this choropter, a spot, will activate corresponding regions in the two eyes.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And then what happens is that those corresponding points go to the same location in the lateral genoclures, in the superior coliculus, and in the visual cortex.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So the logic of this arrangement is to be able to have the two eyes have inputs to corresponding points in the brain. That way you can see something uniform and clear rather than seeing double.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So that is then the arrangement that you have in primates. Now, just anticipate a little bit. Whenever images fall inside or outside the circle, they do follow non-correspondent points, and that will have something to do and I keep you just curious about that for the time being about how we process depth.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that is the mechanism that is involved is called stereopsis. I've mentioned that very b-sheet the last time.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay. So that then summarizes the basic connections of the system. And then what we are going to do next is we're going to go to the retina and look at in more details about how the retina is made.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And then we will go in successive steps to higher areas, sometimes going back and forth to try to understand visual processing in general.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Alright, so now let's begin by looking at the eye. Now, I don't expect you to know all these names here. But you should remember of course the lens and the corner, I know that already. You know the iris, right? What's the iris?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, now what do we base our views on the color of the eye? When you say somebody has blue eyes or somebody has brown eyes. It's based on the fact of what the coloring is of the iris.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Alright, now what's the iris? The iris can get bigger and smaller to control the size of the opening into the lens.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Alright, it's much like what you have in cameras where you have the so-called f-stop. So if you have a large number, say f-16 on a camera, that you set to 16 if you do it manually when it's bright out there.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: But when it's dim out there, like we have in this camera here, you have the lens wide open because it's pretty dark in here. Then you have a low f-stop like an f-4 or even lower to allow more light to come in to more photons to enter the eye.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, next is here's the lens and the fascinating thing about the lenses that in the eye, the lens is totally different from the way it is in a camera.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, maybe by now, many of you don't even know the details about a camera, but one of the details, of course, is that to bring an image into focus, you have to adjust the distance of the lens from the film onto which the image projects.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So the closer an image, the further out the lens has to go. Now, if that were the case in animals and humans, you would have a super-bargaging eye. So when you look at something closely, I would go out like that and that would be really disturbing.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So that is not the case because what happened instead is something very clever that you don't see in cameras, which is that you have this lens here, which is at least in younger people, is an organ that can very in thickness.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So you have here some muscles, the silvery muscles, which can adjust the thickness of this lens to allow it to focus properly on the retina.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, here the retina is going also around, is against the inner wall of the eye, as you all know, that, of course. And to explain how this is done then, very, very, very, very crudely, here we have it.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: If you're an object really near, you want the lens to be thick to properly focus the image on the retina. And if the image is far, you have to thin the lens to get the proper focus on the retina.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So that's how the lens works, at least when you're reasonably young. But then when you get older, what happens is that the lens becomes progressively stiffer and stiffer. And because of that, you have to start wearing glasses.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, in the older days, they used to have lenses, which came in two parts by focus. So if you look in the upper part, you could look in the distance. If you look in the lower part, you could look close.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, they have graduated lenses. So if you look in the upper part, you can look far. And if you want to look some, you're going to look at down below. And then you can see that in focus.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So once you get older, these lenses don't adjust as much, still a little bit. But the proof that this is not that important, thank God, now that we have these graduated lenses and we have bifurcles, is that there are a lot of people in old age who develop, and sometimes not even in old age.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: In India, for example, there are a lot of young kids who have cataracts. Now, what is cataracts? Cataracts interferes with the processing of vision through the lens system. Things become unclear because all kinds of blockage occur in the lens itself.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So how is that corrected? The way that's corrected nowadays, that you have what is called cataract surgery. Got it? Cataract surgery.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So what they do then is they actually literally remove the lens from the eye, and they put in a fixed lens made out of plastic or glass.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And then obviously, since there's no adjustment, you do have to wear glasses to look at things far away and look at things close. And most of the time, those would be sort of graduated lenses.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And so people who have cataract surgery can see extremely well, certainly a lot, lot better than having to try to deal with lenses that have cataracts.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now, there's one other point I want to make about the eye here before we move on. Maybe two points. One is that about this part of the eye, that you have here your so-called iris.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And what do you call what's in the center of the iris? Pupil.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Pupil, very good. Now, what is the color of the pupil? In most people, it's black, right? Why is it black? How can the pupil is black? And he thoughts about that?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, well, let me tell you, and I think what I'll do is actually I'll move on. First, I'll leave this unanswered for just a few minutes. This I've shown you before, which is another amazing thing about the eye, is namely that in the foveeer, you have something like 200,000 cones per square millimeter.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: 200,000. As you go five degrees out, it reduces 10 fold to 20,000. And then you go 10 degrees, it reduces by half. So because of this very high density, I've mentioned this before, in the foveeer, you have high acuity.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And that is one of the prime reasons you have to move your eye around so that you can see things in fine detail. All right, so now let's take another step here and look at the retina itself.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And let me first of all point out to you, I have reversed the image here to have it upside right side up. The light actually comes in from the bottom, goes through all these cells until it's a photoreceptors.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Here are the photoreceptors, and that the closest point to the eye, what we have is called the pigment epithelium.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, so what's the pigment epithelium? The pigment epithelium is a single layer of cells that are pigmented to such a degree that when photons come into the eye and get to the pigment epithelium, they get absorbed just like a black surface that you see as a black surface absorbs the photons and doesn't reflect them.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So because of that, there is no reflection back out. And that explains why the pupil looks black.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, because the light that comes into the eye doesn't reflect back out.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now there's a good reason for that because if it were to reflect back out, it would scatter and it would activate many more photoreceptors than it should and because that you get blurred vision.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now how do we know this? All right.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Can you think of any one or any species of animal where the pigment epithelium is not black?

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, in cat.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So there are some animals, you see mostly animals that specialize in nocturnal vision that have a pigment epithelium which actually has purposefully reflecting molecules in it.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, for them this is called actually the topitum and the purpose of that is to improve your ability to see so that the photons activate the photoreceptors not only in one direction but also when they bounce back.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now the topitum is arranged in such a way most animals that it doesn't freely scatter the light but scatters it in a fairly directional manner and because of that you can see pretty well.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now I'm sure that many of you have noticed that when you drive at night in a deserted area in the United States which are decreasing of course.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: You can see sometimes deer on the highway or rabbits for that matter and when you see them it looks like they have two flashlights projecting at you.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: It's almost scary but it also helps you by being able to avoid hitting them.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now that's because they have that reflecting topitum.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Let's look at a different example. What other human can you think of who does not have a black pigment epithelium or for that matter some animals as well.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Very good. Our binos. Now what's our binos? Our binos are individuals or animals who lack pigments in their skin and end of course in their retina.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So if you look at the eye of an albino actually their pupil is not black their pupil is reddish. Very good. Why is it reddish? Because you have thousands of

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: red in the retina right the right of law especially mostly on the outside here as well. You have blood vessels.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: And the blood vessels, since they carry blood when light reflects from them they will have a reddish color to them and thereby endow if you will the pupil with a reddish color.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now the proof that the main function of the pigment epithelium is to absorb the incoming light to prevent scattering is in the fact that albinoes that lack this pigment epithelium have very poor vision.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: They often have vision to 20 over 200 or something because the light scatters from the epithelium which is no longer pigmented in these people and activates many photoreceptors there by reducing the ability to see things clearly and sharply.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now let's go through this retina here first of all we have the rods and the cones. Now this brings me to a very interesting story at least for me an interesting story.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Back in the 19th century there was a person whose name was, let's see, his name was Max Schulze I'm trying to remember when he lived he lived from 1825 to 1874.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: He developed some new anatomical procedures and as I mentioned before whenever new techniques emerge are invented it's likely that you're going to make some interesting new discoveries.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: He was the first person by then looking at the retina and looking at the photoreceptors that he said my god there are two different kinds of cones.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: I shouldn't say cones, so different kinds of photoreceptors the rods and the cones.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: The rods were sort of like the naming plies rods and the cones were sort of as the naming plies, cone is looking.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So he looked at that and looked at that and said my god why on earth would we have in most animals rods and cones what is their function.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: This was a totally new discovery and when he first published this nobody believed him. They said oh they just vary randomly and some are more rod like some are more cone like but it's just the same photoreceptors.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So he of course did not accept that he saw by being very careful about it that it was two definitely different classes just like it is in this picture.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So he began to ask the question why on earth do we have this. So imagine yourself in the 19th century coming up with this incredible discovery and trying to figure out why we have them.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now if you're a really good scientist then you start to think analytically and so what did he do? I'm so impressed to this day.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: He said well let me take a closer look at the retina and see how the rods and the cones are distributed on the retinal surface and he discovered to go back to the previous slide that there is a small region here the four-year where there are only cones.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: There are no rods and I'll show you a better picture of that in a minute. He said my god if that's the case there must be something different about the way we see in the four-year then we can see in areas where you have lots of rods.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So what did he discover? He discovered that in the four-year we don't see too well at night. And the base of that he concluded that the rods are for night vision.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now this was absolutely incredible. There's only one sad part in this story which is that this discovery was made in the early 19th century before the Nobel Prize was initiated.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Because had the Nobel Prize been initiated before he made this discovery there's no question that he would have received the Nobel Prize for it. There's such an incredible magnitude of discovery.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Alright so let's go back here then and I pointed out the rods and the cones in the pigment epithelium. Now let's go through here and we'll tell you about the rest of the elements and then this time and also in several subsequent sessions we will talk about some of these elements in much more detail especially about the retinal ganglion cells.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: So anyway when you come here to the next stage here we have the so-called horizontal cells and bipolar cells. It's been established that the photoreceptors connect with both of these and then the bipolar cells connect into the inner portion of the retina just show you in a minute.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now this region here is called the OPL and that's easy to remember outer plexiform layer. Now then when you get into the deeper parts of the retina you have the so-called emichin cells and ganglion cells and they reside in the inner plexiform layer.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now it is the ganglion cells that project out of the eye and then project as we had shown you two various regions in the brain most notably that we're going to talk about today, the lateral geniclet nucleus.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
chunks.text: Now let me also point out at this stage, let me see, do I have a picture of that? Point out to you some numbers. Here is an enlarged, highly simplified view of rods.

chunks.title: Lecture8.mp3
